behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler+signature verifier+queue handoff, not a single pure function. Lower-level miss-proof: a Unit test of HMAC compare can pass while wiring still forwards invalid requests. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text only requires 401 + no forward, with no real network/OS timing condition.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with real middleware wiring and a fake queue sink. Send a payload with an intentionally bad `X-Signature-256`. Assert status 401 and assert queue `enqueue` was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across modules (handler and persistent queue). Lower-level miss-proof: Unit-testing handler branches cannot catch real write-before-ack ordering across queue adapter + response path. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text does not require real network/subprocess, only module ordering with persistent storage dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process app wiring with the real queue repository (local test DB/filesystem). Instrument queue write completion and response send. Assert write commit timestamp/event occurs before 200 response is emitted.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at handoff boundary between request path and async processor. Lower-level miss-proof: Unit tests of async helper logic won’t prove response path decouples from processor completion. Rejected higher level (System): no required real OS/network phenomenon; decoupling can be validated in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream worker in-process. Make worker intentionally block (e.g., unresolved promise or delayed completion). Assert HTTP response is returned immediately after enqueue and before worker completion.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Evidence: explicit 'within 3 seconds wall-clock' under concurrent slow workload. Lower-level miss-proof: mocked timers/in-process-only fakes can hide event-loop contention, socket/backlog, and scheduler effects that cause real SLA misses.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service as a real process and send real HTTP traffic: saturate with earlier slow events, then send a fresh valid webhook and measure end-to-end wall-clock latency. Assert response < 3s under defined concurrency/load profile.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at module/state boundary (handler + persistent dedup store/queue). Lower-level miss-proof: Unit tests of ID-check helper can pass while persistence uniqueness/upsert wiring still allows duplicates. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text does not require real network/OS; local persistent store integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Use real dedup/persistence layer in test environment. Send two requests with same event ID and valid signatures. Assert first is enqueued, second is acknowledged as duplicate path, and queue contains exactly one record for that ID.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is contract mapping between queue backpressure signal and HTTP response semantics. Lower-level miss-proof: Unit test of status-code mapping alone can pass while integration wiring fails to propagate 'full' condition/header. Rejected higher level (System): no real network/subprocess dependency in the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module configured to return a concrete 'full' error. Send valid webhook and assert 503 plus `Retry-After` header value from policy/config.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is queue logic + persistence backend enforcing capacity state. Lower-level miss-proof: Unit tests of counter math can pass while repository transaction/constraint behavior lets overflow through. Rejected higher level (System): requirement is local persistence enforcement, not real external network/OS primitive behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend with small capacity (e.g., 2). Enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more. Assert over-capacity write is rejected and persisted queue size remains at configured maximum.
